# Transcrição: 12-eaving Clara at 7-11

- **Fonte:** 12-eaving Clara at 7-11
- **Duração:** 06:37
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:02]

I'm standing in front of my kitchen sink. There's a steel colander in the middle of the sink. And inside that colander is my eight day, eight day old baby daughter Clara. I'm giving her a bath. I'm going to surprise my wife. She's upstairs taking her first shower after her C-section. And I decided I'm going to give the baby a bath so when she gets out she can be excited. I know this is a DIY situation going on in the sink and it's working. It's okay. I have to hold the colander because it is not steady with a baby in it. But I've got the hose and I'm hosing the baby off. And I've got a little towel so I'm washing the baby. She's beautiful. And when it's done it works. She's clean. I put her in a towel and I wrap her up like a burrito and I put her in the drying rack so that I can clean the colander. And it's empty except for it has one Tupperware container. I didn't plan on this. But it holds up her head just perfectly. So she's just watching me clean out the colander when I hear, why is my baby in the drying rack? And I turn and it's my wife. She's got a towel on and her hair is wet. I know what happened. Her alarm went off. She realized my baby has been alone with the idiot downstairs for 10 minutes. So something bad is happening. And I said, I gave her a bath. It works. And she says, why didn't you use the baby tub? And I think, I've lived here for seven years. We don't have a baby tub. We've got one tub. It turns out we have a baby tub. It's that blue thing that's been in the bathroom for like seven months. I didn't know what it was. She doesn't tell me any of that. She just takes the baby and goes upstairs. And I stand there feeling foolish and stupid, which is how I often feel when I'm dealing with this baby. And it's not how I planned it. I really thought we were going to crush this. When my wife was pregnant, people would come up to us and people who have kids who are like two and 12 and 20, and they would say things to us like, get all the sleep you can because as soon as that baby's born, you're never going to get a good night's sleep again. Or go to the movies now. You're never going to leave the house after that baby's born. And I always thought two things when I heard that. First was what kind of sick failure of a human being goes up to a pregnant woman and says the baby growing inside you is going to ruin your life. I hated these people. But my second thought was no, it's not going to happen to us because we're going to crush this. And we sort of do. Our baby sleeps beautifully. Clara is a great sleeper, not really because of us, but because I went out and I bought this monstrosity of a crib. It is huge and misshapen and it's olive green. It looks like it belongs on a battlefield instead of a bedroom. But at the time, it's the only crib on the market that has a button you can press and it will rock the baby all night long all by itself.

## [03:02 - 06:03]

And so she sleeps like a baby. And for the movies, we waited for the summer to come and we went to drive in movies and Clara would sleep in the back and we would sit in the front. And when she woke up for food, at least she would take a boob out and she would nurse the baby and no one would know we saw 50 movies that summer. Movies we did not want to see. Movies we hated just to screw the people who told us it wasn't going to happen. So we're crushing it, but it's a we. Whenever it's me, it doesn't go well. I'm just not good at any of this. I can't tell the difference between regular clothes and pajamas. So when my wife says, can you go get her dressed, I bring her back downstairs and Alicia says, those are also pajamas. Try again. And the number of times I forget to put a diaper on. And you can just imagine a woman nursing a baby and suddenly there's diarrhea. And the only thing between her and the baby is like a thin layer of cotton. And there's a time when I get the clothing right and I tried to choose for the first time and I put these sandals on Clara's feet, but I put them on the wrong feet. I swear I don't see it, but her pinky toe comes out at a 90 degree angle from the shoe. So when Alicia sees it, she screams. And if the baby's bones weren't so pliable, it would have knocked right off. And so I'm worried because I'm really bad and Alicia's really good and I'm jealous of how she has all the information already downloaded. She's never had a baby before, but she knows when to call the doctor and when not to call the doctor and what the baby needs. And she can tell which crime means what. And I know none of it. And I know that Clara's going to get old enough eventually to realize I'm a terrible parent and she's going to love Alicia more than she loves me. And that makes sense, but I don't like it. And so we're like five weeks in and Alicia is ready to go out with her friends to have dinner and she's leaving me alone with the baby. And I'm nervous because I make a lot of mistakes, but I feed Clara and I read to her and I rock her and I get her into the crib and I press the button so it begins rocking and it goes so well. Honestly, it's better than Alicia has ever done it. And I decide I'm going to tell her, I know it's not going to be good for me, but I need this. I need, I need this victory. And so I go downstairs, I'm a writer, I start working on my book and I'm just waiting for her to come home and I decide this is a victory. I deserve something. And so I go to the door and I get my coat and I put it on and I grab my keys and I go to my car and I back out of the driveway and I go all the way down my street and I take a left and I go all the way up that street. And it's when I can see the sign to 7-Eleven where I can get my chunky monkey that I go oh, and I realize I have just left my baby at home alone. And if we're really being honest, I kind of forgot she existed for a moment.

## [06:03 - 06:37]

And as I'm driving back, like a race car driver, I have to debate whether I'm going to tell Alicia that I have done this or not because there's no one in the world who knows I've done it. Clara cannot tell on me. But before I even get home, I decide I have to tell her because this is not going to be the first of many tragic mistakes. And I'm either going to build this relationship on truth and I will admit to every mistake I make or I will have to lie for the rest of my life. And she knows what she married and somehow she loves it anyway. And I'm going to go ahead with that. Thank you.
